coredata.dc:title: Model-based optimal experimental design for lithium-ion battery aging: Insights from a comprehensive multi-stage study with real-world data

coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion battery degradation presents challenges for aging characterization due to various stress factors and lengthy testing periods. Optimal experimental design (OED) techniques can enhance study efficiency by maximizing information gain with minimum number of experiments. This study evaluates the efficiency of parameter-individual OED (pi-OED) in calibrating a lithium-ion battery aging model using a multi-stage framework. Analyzing a dataset from 279 commercial cells tested under 71 different conditions, parameter-individual OED is compared with traditional approaches for calendar and cycle aging. For calendar aging, pi-OED reduces uncertainty in the temperature-dependent model parameters by 28% compared to a non-optimized experimental design, demonstrating significant efficiency gains. However, when determining cyclic aging parameters, pi-OED cannot reduce the parameter uncertainty of the underlying heuristic model. This can be explained by its inadequacy, which predicts a model sensitivity that could not be confirmed by experiments. This highlights the necessity of a valid model for successful OED applications. We provide a structured framework for OED in the field of battery aging, addressing the key challenges in terms of effectiveness and efficiency. Our work guides efficient battery characterization experiments and underscores the limitations of model-based optimization when model fidelity or parameter significance are insufficient.

head.ce:title: Model-based optimal experimental design for lithium-ion battery aging: Insights from a comprehensive multi-stage study with real-world data

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Sustainable Energy Systems (ISES), Munich University of Applied Sciences, Lothstr. 64, 80335 Munich, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion battery degradation presents challenges for aging characterization due to various stress factors and lengthy testing periods. Optimal experimental design (OED) techniques can enhance study efficiency by maximizing information gain with minimum number of experiments. This study evaluates the efficiency of parameter-individual OED (pi-OED) in calibrating a lithium-ion battery aging model using a multi-stage framework. Analyzing a dataset from 279 commercial cells tested under 71 different conditions, parameter-individual OED is compared with traditional approaches for calendar and cycle aging. For calendar aging, pi-OED reduces uncertainty in the temperature-dependent model parameters by 28% compared to a non-optimized experimental design, demonstrating significant efficiency gains. However, when determining cyclic aging parameters, pi-OED cannot reduce the parameter uncertainty of the underlying heuristic model. This can be explained by its inadequacy, which predicts a model sensitivity that could not be confirmed by experiments. This highlights the necessity of a valid model for successful OED applications. We provide a structured framework for OED in the field of battery aging, addressing the key challenges in terms of effectiveness and efficiency. Our work guides efficient battery characterization experiments and underscores the limitations of model-based optimization when model fidelity or parameter significance are insufficient.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data used in this study has already been published. The data descriptor is referenced in the publication.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become essential in various applications, ranging from portable electronics to large-scale stationary battery energy storage systems (BESS) [1–3]. As LIB and BESS demand continues to grow, understanding their degradation behavior is crucial to improving their performance, lifespan, and safety [3–5]. This aging characterization requires extensive experimental campaigns tailored to the specific cell type and target application. Such studies are often time- and equipment-intensive and expensive, thereby necessitating the development of efficient experimental design methodologies [6–10].

The characterization of battery aging is complicated by the multiple of interacting factors. Capacity loss and resistance increase – the primary manifestations of degradation – are thought to be influenced by environmental (such as temperature) and operational conditions (such as state of charge, depth of discharge, charge and discharge currents) or by cumulative usage metrics (such as equivalent full cycles, calendar time) and their interactions [6,7,9–12]. In the case of wide-spread parametric models [6,9,13,14], capturing these dependencies requires well-designed experiments that explore the multidimensional parameter space to effectively provide information for an estimation of the model parameter values.

A majority of battery aging studies rely on non-model-based design of experiments (DoE) approaches such as full-factorial (FF) and one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) designs (e.g., in [15–17]). However, these DoE methods become inefficient when prior knowledge about the system exists [18], or effectively fail to fully capture n-fold interactions between influencing factors if the number of experiments is limited [19]. In contrast, model-based optimal experimental design (OED) methodologies leverage existing information to select experiments that maximize information gain for effective model parameter estimation while efficiently minimizing its related experimental effort [20,21].

The concept of OED has its roots in the pioneering work of statisticians such as Ronald A. Fisher, George Box, Jack Kiefer and Jacob Wolfowitz in the early to mid-20th century [22,23]. They laid the mathematical foundations for optimizing experimental designs to maximize the information content from a limited number of experiments with respect to a given model. The OED concept has been successfully transferred to a variety of application fields [8,13,24]. Despite its potential advantages, the application of OED in the field of LIB aging is still limited. Some studies have employed OED for specific aspects of battery testing, such as optimizing fast-charging protocols [25] or designing cycle aging experiments [9]. However, a comprehensive evaluation of OED methodologies for LIB aging studies considering both calendar and cycle aging factors has not yet been conducted.

Furthermore, the efficiency gain of OED approaches in real LIB aging studies has not yet been validated on a comprehensive level. While simulation studies [13,26] have demonstrated the potential of OED to improve parameter estimation accuracy, experimental validation is still pending to assess effectiveness and efficiency under practical constraints and uncertainties. Such validation requires a systematic comparison of OED-based experiments with traditional DoE approaches using a consistent set of cells and test conditions.

The study on hand aims to statistically evaluate the efficiency gain and the admissibility of OED techniques in the context of LIB aging characterization. Its primary objective is to quantify and validate the impact of OED on parameter estimation quality compared to traditional non-model-based DoE approaches. This is achieved by applying the previously published multi-stage OED (MS-OED) framework [26]. This framework combines initial exploratory stages based on FF and conditioned LH (cLH) sampling. Subsequent stages utilize either parameter-individual OED (pi-OED), or for comparison, the same non-model-based approaches used in the initial stage. The previously presented results provide the appropriate database for this purpose [27].
The key contributions and unique aspects of this study include:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Quantification of OED gain: The study provides a quantification of the benefits and challenges of applying OED techniques to LIB aging characterization, considering both calendar and cycle aging factors in comparison to FF and cLH sampling approaches.

Multi-Stage Experimental Framework: By employing a multi-stage approach, the study demonstrates how initial exploratory stages can inform the design of subsequent OED stages, enabling efficient refinement of model parameters.

Parameter-Individual OED (pi-OED): The study for the first time applies pi-OED to optimize the experimental design for minimum uncertainty of individually selectable model parameters under given experimental constraints.

Validation with Real-World Data: The effectiveness of the proposed pi-OED methodology is validated using comprehensive experimental data from a large-scale LIB aging study, for the first time demonstrating its practical relevance.
Critical Assessment of OED Limitations: A balanced assessment based on published models and data highlights admissibility and limits of the OED approach, providing crucial insights for practitioners.

ce:section.ce:para: The method presented here is examined in aging tests on battery cells with constant-current load profiles and is applied on a predefined semi-heuristic aging model. Topics such as the path dependency of aging and the influence of highly dynamic load profiles as well as parameter and state inhomogeneities within battery packs on aging have not yet been taken into account in favor of clarity and stringency of the method.

The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Section 2 provides an overview of the methodology, including the statistical modeling concepts, design of experiments approaches, and the multi-stage experimental framework. Section 3 presents the application of the proposed methodology to two real-world LIB aging studies, focusing on calendar and cycle aging, respectively. The results are interpreted and discussed in Section 4, highlighting the efficiency gain and admissibility of OED in each case study. Finally, Section 5 concludes the paper, summarizing the key findings and outlining future research directions.

In this study, the multi-stage experimental design framework in absence of system pre-knowledge [26] is applied to published real data from an aging study [27] to evaluate the efficiency and admissibility of OED techniques for LIB aging characterization. The methodology combines non-model-based DoE approaches in initial stages with model-based pi-OED in subsequent stages. The multi-stage approach addresses a fundamental challenge in OED application, which requires a usually non-existing knowledge of parameter values at the beginning of an aging study, and shows new approaches to solving it.

By comparing the results of pi-OED stages with parallel non-model-based reference stages, the impact of OED on parameter estimation quality under identical experimental conditions is quantified. The following subsections provide the statistical foundations, required definitions and DoE approaches including a description of the evaluation methodology employed in this study.

In practice, the true parameter vector

is generally unknown and must be inferred from experimental data. The inference may be obtained using a parameter estimator for θ , which is defined as a measurable function
T : Ω → Θ

Two critical properties characterize a useful estimator for practical applications: (a) it should be unbiased, i.e. converge to the true value as the sample size increases, and (b) it should possess minimum variance, i.e. reveal minimal uncertainty in the estimates. Both criteria are satisfied by the class of estimators known as Minimum Variance Unbiased Estimators (MVUE) [28]. The Maximum Likelihood Estimator (MLE) is widely accepted and, under mild conditions, coincides with or approximates such an MVUE [13].

For normally distributed measurement noise, the MLE simplifies to the Least Mean Squares (LMS) estimator [28–30]. This estimator (denoted by a hat symbol) can be formulated as the following optimization problem:

While estimators allow a parameter approximation, they are also subject to uncertainty due to an underlying experimental noise. Accordingly, in experimental studies it is not sufficient to pay attention only to the estimates themselves, but also to the associated uncertainties or a confidence measure of their significance. This work uses two complementary methodologies to quantify parameter uncertainty: Fisher Information (FI) and bootstrapping (BS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fisher information and Cramer–Rao lower bound.
ce:section.ce:para:
The FI provides a measure of the amount of information that observable data carries about unknown parameters within a given model. FI serves as a means for quantifying the uncertainty of parameter estimators, particularly within the context of MLE. For a parametric model with a probability measure (or likelihood function)

The FIM quantifies how sensitive the likelihood function is to changes in the parameters for a given experiment. Intuitively, if the likelihood function sharply peaks around the true parameter value, the FI is large, indicating that small changes in the data provide substantial information about θ . Conversely, a flat likelihood function corresponds to low FI, indicating high uncertainty in parameter estimation.

A fundamental relation derived from the FI is the Cramer–Rao Lower Bound (CRLB), which provides a theoretical asymptotic lower bound on the co-variance of any unbiased estimator

Bootstrapping is a statistical resampling method that complements the model-based FI approach by providing an empirical assessment of parameter uncertainty. This technique is particularly valuable when the underlying distribution assumptions may not fully hold or when evaluating real-world experimental data.

In bootstrapping, a large number
n
BS
of resampled datasets are generated from the original data by random sampling with replacement. Each bootstrap sample maintains the same size as the original dataset, but some observations may appear multiple times while others may not appear at all. For each bootstrap sample, the parameter estimation procedure is repeated, resulting in a distribution of
n
BS

A key advantage of bootstrapping is that it makes minimal assumptions about the underlying distribution of the data, providing a robust assessment of parameter uncertainty that can validate or complement the theoretical predictions from the FIM.

In blockwise bootstrapping, connected data blocks are drawn instead of individual observations to account for dependency structures within the data. This approach is particularly valuable for time-dependent data or time series where adjacent observations exhibit correlation [33].

DoE methodologies are essential for efficient parameter estimation in complex systems. The quality and reliability of parameter estimates significantly depend on how experiments are designed to collect informative data. A well-structured experimental design is essential to ensure that the collected data maximizes information about the parameters while minimizing required experimental resources.
DoE methodologies can be broadly categorized into non-model-based and model-based approaches, each with distinct advantages depending on the available prior knowledge about the system.

Non-model-based methods do not require prior knowledge about the system or its parameters, making them suitable for initial exploratory stages when little is known about the system behavior or parameter values.

Full Factorial (FF) Design is one of the most comprehensive approaches to DoE [34]. In this method, experiments are conducted at all possible combinations of factor levels. For a system with n factors, each at
l
i
levels, the total number of experiments is
k =
∏
i = 1
n
l
i
. This approach ensures that all main effects and interactions between factors may be explored, providing a systematic picture of system behavior across the experimental space. However, the exponential growth in required experiments with increasing factors and levels often makes FF design impractical for complex systems with many factors. [35]

Latin Hypercube (LH) Sampling offers a more efficient alternative for high-dimensional experimental spaces with reduced variance of statistical estimates [36]. Within the LH sampling stratification approach, the range of each factor is divided into k intervals, and one sample is taken from each interval. These samples are then combined to provide uniform coverage of the experimental space with fewer experiments than FF design. LH sampling is particularly advantageous when many factors shall be investigated with a limited number of available samples. [37]

Conditioned Latin Hypercube (cLH) Sampling extends LH sampling to accommodate constraints and discrete factors that are often present in real-world applications [38]. It optimizes sample selection to form a Latin hypercube while ensuring that samples satisfy specific constraints and the discrete nature of certain factors. This method begins with a full factorial grid of the experimental design space, considering all constraints and discretization requirements, then selects a subset of points that optimally represent the space while respecting all constraints [39].

When prior information about the system model is available, model-based DoE approaches can significantly improve the efficiency of parameter estimation by focusing experimental resources on the most informative regions of the parameter space.

Optimal Experimental Design (OED) uses a parametric model with an initial parameter estimate
θ
est
to guide the selection of experiments. The methodical base of OED is to minimize the variance of parameter estimators by optimizing scalarized criteria based on the FIM. Several such optimization criteria have been proposed:

D-optimality minimizes the determinant of the inverse FIM:

This criterion effectively minimizes the volume of the confidence ellipsoid for the parameter estimates, providing balanced improvement across all parameters.

Parameter-Individual OED (pi-OED) minimizes the uncertainty of an individually selectable parameter or parameter interaction of interest:
(12)

where the i th diagonal element of the inverse FIM corresponds to the variance of the i th parameter estimate while off-diagonal elements represent according parameter covariances. This approach is particularly valuable when certain parameters are of specific interest or have higher uncertainty than others.

By solving these optimization problems, an experimenter can select experimental designs that yield the most informative data for specific parameters of interest while reducing parameter uncertainty compared to non-optimized designs.

The choice of DoE methodology should be guided by the specific goals of the study and the level of prior knowledge about the system. Non-model-based methods like FF design and (c)LH sampling are suitable when little is known about the system, while OED approaches use existing knowledge to focus resources on the most informative experiments, enhancing estimation efficiency and accuracy.
The MS-OED framework [26] integrates the strengths of both non-model-based and model-based approaches.
The MS-OED framework enables:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Initial exploration of the experimental space in the absence of parameter value knowledge using non-model-based methods (Stage S1)
Parameter estimation based on the evaluation of initial exploratory data of S1
Design optimization for subsequent stages using model-based OED informed by initial parameter estimates
Validation of OED in comparison to non-optimized experimental designs

ce:section.ce:para: In this framework, the exploratory character of non-model-based DoE methodologies like FF and cLH is leveraged to identify initial parameter estimates. These estimates then inform subsequent OED stages, which can exploit this knowledge for an optimized model confidence. By staging experiments this way, the framework efficiently bridges the gap between exploration and optimization.

Assume that the parameters of a given parametric model are to be identified using experimental results. To validate the efficiency of OED in experimental planning against non-OED based approached, the results of two series of experiments with the same total duration and number of trials must be compared. To realize those two series of experiments, the evaluation process in Fig. 2 introduces three experimental setup cases, that represent the parameter estimation of a given parametric model based on different datasets:

ce:def-description.ce:para: S1 + second stage (S2a) with non-model-based DoE
S1 + second stage (S2b) with model-based OED

The OED methodology can be considered validated if the parameter uncertainty in Case 3 is lower than that in Case 2:

In other words: If the standard deviation of the estimator for the i th parameter is smaller when using experiments designed with OED compared to non-model-based DoE methods, then OED can be stated to have a proven added value of efficiency for parameter calibration in comparison with non-model based DoE approaches.

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Remark
ce:enunciation.ce:para: The OED added value prediction on theoretical basis is founded on the following key assumptions, derived from the analyses and descriptions by S. M. Kay [28] and H.-O. Georgii [30]:

ce:list-item.ce:label: R1.
ce:list-item.ce:para:
The parametric model function
f
( x ,
θ
true
)
functionally reflects the true behavior of the system under test without redundancy.

ce:list-item.ce:label: R2.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Results of repeated experiments follow a homoscedastic normal distribution around the model prediction.

ce:list-item.ce:label: R3.
ce:list-item.ce:para: The stage S1 parameter estimates are sufficiently close to the true value to ensure the effectiveness of the calculated OED.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of OED validity for LIB aging studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The aging of lithium-ion batteries is commonly modeled as the linear superposition of calendar aging and cycle aging components
Q
rel
cal
and
Q
rel
cyc
, respectively:
(17)
Q
rel
=
Q
rel
cal
+
Q
rel
cyc
with
Q
rel
describing the relative capacity with respect to initial capacity. This assumption, while simplified compared to real-world battery degradation where path dependence and interaction effects exist [40], allows to isolate these components by conducting separate aging studies focused on characterizing either calendar or cycle aging. By examining both phenomena independently, the efficacy of pi-OED can be systematically evaluated at different levels of model complexity.

Fig. 3 presents the complete dataset used in this evaluation, showing the relative capacity loss trajectories for all test cells across different experimental stages and conditions. The data clearly reveals characteristic aging patterns with varying degradation rates depending on the specific stress conditions applied.

Data points deviating from the expected trend were cross-referenced with known incidents (see [27]). In total 48 of 1876 data points (2.56%) could be attributed to anomalies in the measurement process and were excluded from model parameterization in the subsequent steps. For the present study, outliers are defined using the criteria described in the originally published publication [27]. This publication provides a detailed analysis of the identified outliers and associated events during the study. Moreover, in order to assure that production batches cause no significant variations in the cell behaviors, all differential voltage curves of the cells have been statistically analyzed, as explained in [27].

Given the semi-empirical calendar aging model as a parametric model function, which was motivated by Muehlbauer et al. [41]:

Temperature factor levels were set to three values (10 °C, 23 °C, and 45 °C) due to limitations in climate chamber availability. SoC factor levels were fixed to five values (5%, 20%, 50%, 80%, and 100%). With 33 available testing channels in stage S1, the FF grid was partially executed with 11 test points out of the potential 15 combinations with 3 repetitions.

The second stage of experiments was split into two parallel sub-stages with equal numbers of samples. Sub-stage S2a used the same FF sampling methodology as stage S1, completing the full factorial grid. Sub-stage S2b employed pi-OED for the temperature related parameter based on the results from stage S1. The resulting experimental designs are shown in Fig. B.9.

The capacity loss results from the calendar aging experiments in stage S1 are presented in Fig. 3a. The trends follow the expected sublinear behavior for capacity degradation, with higher temperatures generally resulting in accelerated degradation (see Fig. C.11a). With increasing SoC, aging increases up to

to conform with assumption in remark R1. Modifying the model to represent the non-monotonic trend would have been an option, but it was a deliberate decision not to change the model to focus on parameterization.

The combined data from Stages S1 and S2a yield the parameter values and standard deviations for the non-OED reference case through MLE and bootstrapping, respectively. These results are summarized in Table D.2. This case represents the benchmark against which the OED approach is evaluated.

The degradation trajectories of cells aged according to the pi-OED in stage S2b are presented in Fig. 3c. The pi-OED experimental design repeats one test point multiple times, resulting in limited variation across the capacity profiles. These results, when combined with stage S1 data, provide the basis for evaluating the effectiveness of the pi-OED approach.

The parameter values and uncertainties obtained through MLE and bootstrapping of the combined data from Stages S1 and S2b (pi-OED) are listed in Table D.2. Comparing these results with those from Case 2 allows us to quantify the impact of pi-OED on parameter estimation quality.

This reduction in uncertainty is achieved with equal experimental resources, confirming the effectiveness of pi-OED for the calendar aging characterization.

Due to the higher dimensionality of the cycle aging problem (five controllable factors), the experimental domain was sampled using cLH sampling [39] rather than FF design. The experimental factors were constrained to practical ranges: 23 °C ≤ T ≤ 45 °C, 0.2 ≤ SoC

With 75 available test channels, cLH was employed to select 25 test points in the first stage S1, with each condition replicated three times to capture cell-to-cell variability. Capacity measurements were conducted periodically during the aging study according to a fixed time schedule.

Similar to the calendar aging study, the second stage of cycle aging experiments was split into two parallel sub-stages with equal numbers of samples. Sub-stage S2a employed the same cLH sampling methodology as stage S1, while sub-stage S2b used pi-OED based on stage S1 results. This process is elaborated in subsequent sections, while the resulting experimental designs are shown in Fig. B.10.

The capacity fade results from stage S1 cycle aging experiments are shown in Fig. 3d. The degradation trajectories exhibit varying rates depending on the specific cycling conditions, with generally sublinear behavior with respect to cycle count. These data provide the basis for initial parameter estimation and uncertainty assessment. 44 of 959 data points (4.59%) were declared outliers and were removed from model parameterization in the subsequent steps according to the procedure described above. For the detailed description of the outlier identification and handling we want to refer to the published dataset [27].

The capacity fade trajectories for the non-OED reference case S2a are displayed in Fig. 3e. These experiments provide additional coverage of the experimental space using the same cLH sampling methodology as stage S1. 1 of 467 data points (0.21%) were declared outliers and were removed from model parameterization in the subsequent steps.

By combining data from Stages S1 and S2a, the model parameters are estimated for the non-OED reference case. The resulting parameter values and uncertainties from blockwise bootstrapping are summarized in Table D.3. This case serves as the benchmark for evaluating the effectiveness of pi-OED in the cycle aging context.

The degradation trends of cells aged according to the pi-OED in stage S2b are presented in Fig. 3f. Notably, some of these cells exhibit accelerated degradation compared to the patterns observed in Stages S1 and S2a. 3 of 450 data points (0.67%) were declared outliers and were removed from model parameterization in the subsequent steps.
The pi-OED approach seems to have selected more stressful operating conditions to maximize information gain about the current-rate parameters.

The parameter values and standard deviations obtained through MLE and blockwise bootstrapping for the combined data from Stages S1 and S2b (pi-OED) are listed in Table D.3. Comparing these results with those from Case 2 allows us to evaluate the effectiveness of pi-OED for cycle aging characterization.

These unexpected results indicate potential limitations in the effectiveness of pi-OED for the cycle aging model. Unlike the calendar aging case, where pi-OED successfully reduced parameter uncertainty, the cycle aging application demonstrates that OED may not always deliver improvements in parameter estimation quality.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effectiveness of OED for calendar aging
ce:section.ce:para:
The results of the calendar aging study validate the efficiency value-add of the pi-OED methodology in reducing parameter uncertainty. In stage S1 (Case 1), the temperature-related parameter
θ
0
cal
was identified as the target for optimization based on its largest relative standard deviation derived from the FIM. The subsequent stage S2 was divided into two parallel sub-stages which result in Case 2 (S1 + S2a non-OED) and Case 3 (S1 + S2b pi-OED) for an admissible direct benchmark. Comparison of the bootstrap-derived standard deviations for parameter
θ
0
cal
reveals a 28% reduction in Case 3 relative to Case 2, clearly confirming the pi-OED efficiency gain.

A counterintuitive observation emerged during analysis: the
θ
0
cal
parameter distributions estimated through bootstrapping differed by more than
4 σ
between Case 1 and the subsequent Cases 2 and 3. This finding is particularly surprising because the datasets for Cases 2 and 3 contained all measurements from Case 1, supplemented with additional experiments. Intuitively, one would expect parameter estimator distributions to overlap when all experiments are conducted identically and independently, the underlying model correctly captures all relevant physical dependencies (remark R1), and the model parameter estimators fully converged after Stage S1 (remark R3). To systematically investigate this phenomenon, a virtual experiment was conducted based on the parametric model
Q
rel
cal
( T , S o C , t ;
θ
cal,true
)
with known parameter vector
θ
cal,true
=
( 5000 , 0 . 87 , 0 . 67 )
and virtual RPTs performed after
t =
[0, 35, 63, 119, 175, 231] days. The experiment followed an iterative approach: first, various DoE alternatives consisting of 5 test points, each repeated 3 times, were generated using LH sampling. For each design, virtual experiments were performed with Gaussian noise (
σ = 0 . 002
) superimposed on the results to simulate real measurement conditions. These results were used for parameter estimation of the identical model, with the resulting parameter distributions analyzed through bootstrapping. The identifiability of the model in general has been analyzed in [26], where the calendar aging model was identifiable for 5 test points after at least 4 RPTs. These numbers are exceeded in the experimental study and in this virtual study.

The objective was to identify two experimental designs whose parameter estimator distributions differ by more than
4 σ
. After 333 iterations, two such distributions were identified, shown in Fig. 8 as iteration index 141 and 333. The results reveal two notable aspects: First, even under ideal conditions (i.e. using the identical model for both simulation and parameterization) distributions can exhibit a separation of
4 σ
. Second, neither resulting distribution precisely captures the true parameter value
θ
cal,true
, which indicates a non-converged parameter estimator (violated assumption R3).

Additionally, the disparities in variance estimates between bootstrapping and FIM in stage S1 provide an important insight: they may indicate a not yet fully converged parameter estimator, underscoring the necessity for additional experiments, as the variance estimation through CRLB and FIM asymptotically approaches the true variance, given the correct model (R1). This is particularly relevant because the FIM-based variance estimation depends on the estimated parameter value and only yields accurate values when based on the true parameter
θ
t r u e
. However, the calculation of the OED experimental plan is only marginally affected by the non-convergence argument in our specific application and model, as a sensitivity analysis of the parameter estimation errors revealed in [26]. The conclusions regarding variance reduction through pi-OED, based on uncertainties derived from bootstrapping, remain valid, even if the absolute parameter values may not have completely converged.

To assess whether measurement errors followed a normal distribution (remark R2), Shapiro–Wilk normality tests [43] were conducted across all RPTs and experimental cases. Statistical analysis using a significance level of
α = 0 . 05
support that measurement errors in the calendar aging data follow a normal distribution. The detailed test results are presented in Fig. E.12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges with OED for the cycle aging model
ce:section.ce:para:
In contrast to the calendar aging study, the cycle aging investigation reveals several potential limitations and challenges in applying pi-OED methodology. From FIM analysis, in stage S1 (Case 1), the current rate-related parameters
θ
2
and
θ
3
were identified as the targets for optimization based on their large relative standard deviation with respect to other parameters. The reduction in standard deviation of the estimator for
θ
2
in Case 3 (S1 + S2b pi-OED) compared to the non-OED Case 2 (S1 + S2b non-OED), however, was substantially less pronounced (with reference to Case 1). On the contrary, the standard deviation in the optimized Case 3 is approximately 17-fold higher than in Case 2, whereas, for the
θ
3
estimator, the standard deviations actually deteriorated in both Cases 2 and 3 compared to Case 1. This contradicts the expectation that optimization of experimental designs should reduce standard deviations compared to conventional DoE methods. Furthermore, adding new experiments should always lead to a reduction in estimator variance [13]. At first glance, the results therefore appear puzzling.

The findings for the cycle aging model parameter calibration indicate that the pi-OED approach was neither effective in reducing the uncertainty of the selected parameters overall nor efficient in terms of an OED superiority with respect to the estimator variance when compared to the cLH approach. This brings us back to the concluding remarks of Section 2, which list the requirements for the permissible applicability of the OED methodology. Since a substantial effect due to deviation from the true parameter (remark R3) could be ruled out due to its low sensitivity on the pi-OED calculation according [26,27], the question of model validity (remark R1) is of primary importance. Therefore, this hypothesis of inadequate modeling of the current rate-dependent cycle aging behavior will be discussed first in the following.

Effects not captured by the model can limit the ability of OED to improve parameter estimates. For instance, the superlinear aging trajectories evident in Fig. 3f are not represented in this form by the model. Model evaluation metrics such as
R
adj
2
, root mean square error (RMSE), and mean absolute error (MAE), whose calculation methods are defined in Appendix G, enable assessment of model validity. An
R
adj
2
> 0 . 95
combined with an
M A E
in the magnitude of the measurement error would indicate a valid model [13]. As shown in Table 1, neither of the two criteria, however, is met for the cycle aging model (standard deviation of real experiments:
σ
exp
= 0 . 00274
). This can be regarded as a first strong indication of insufficient model validity with regard to the representation of current-dependent aging.

Beyond model validity and in the sense of a further hypothesis to explain the results, the sensitivity of the current-rate, associated with the parameters
θ
2
and
θ
3
, to the model output may not be sufficient to be effectively influenced through experimental design optimization. This hypothesis can be tested using a Leave-one-covariate-out (LOCO) feature importance method, which investigates the influence of specific factors on model performance by deliberately omitting one or more influencing factors [44]. For this purpose, the original cycle aging model in Eq. (21) was compared with a reduced model function that excludes the influence of the charging and discharging current rates
C
c h , d c h
: with a reduced model function that excludes the influence of charge and discharge current rates
C
c h , d c h
:
(23)

When this model is parameterized for cases 1–3 analogous to the approach in Section 3.2, the model evaluation metrics
R
adj
2
, RMSE, and MAE shown in Table 1 are obtained.

To compare the two models, the distributions of residuals between model predictions and experimental data were analyzed. The null hypothesis
H
0
states, that the residual distributions from both models originate from the same population. In simpler terms, this corresponds to a test of whether adding the features
C
ch
and
C
dch
enables a better model to explain a higher proportion of the residuals. If the residual distributions differ significantly,
H
0
can be rejected; otherwise,
H
0
cannot be rejected. For hypothesis testing, the Mann–Whitney U (MWU) approach [45] is applied, i.e. a non-parametric test that makes no assumptions about the underlying distribution function. Consequently, normal distribution of residuals is not required for its permissible use.

A significance level of
α = 0 . 05
was established for the hypothesis test. Since a null hypothesis can only be rejected but never confirmed, the correlation between residual distributions of both models using the non-parametric Spearman rank correlation coefficient
ρ
s
was additionally quantified. This metric makes no assumptions about the distribution of the variables under consideration and does not presume a linear relationship. For
ρ
s
= 0
, the samples are uncorrelated, while for
ρ
s
= 1
, the samples are identical [46].

The reduced model yielded slightly worse results for Cases 1 and 2, and marginally better results for Case 3, with differences being minimal across all cases examined. With
p − M W U > 0 . 05
in all three cases,
H
0
cannot be rejected at a significance level of
α = 0 . 05
. Consequently, the differences between the models are not statistically significant at this level. In addition, the Spearman rank correlation coefficient of
ρ
s
> 0 . 995
in all three cases confirms virtually identical distributions of residuals from both models. Therefore, the features
C
ch
and
C
dch
do not contribute significantly to explaining the residuals. The influence of current rates on aging is not statistically significant and thus are not justified on experimental base to be represented in the model. 2
2 This does not necessarily mean that aging is not sensitive to current rate at all, but rather that (within the tested operating range) the sensitivity to current rate might be masked by modeling inaccuracies and measurement noise. Consequently, neither an effectivity nor an efficiency effect of pi-OED on parameters associated with such non-sensitive influencing factors may be expected. Accordingly, the measurement results for the cycle aging model also agree well with the theoretical predictions, even if only in terms of confirming the need to fulfill necessary conditions for a permissible application of the methodology (remarks R1-R3). This finding motivates the recommendation that in future experimental studies using the pi-OED approach, the model and the influencing factors should be evaluated after the initial phase and pi-OED should only be applied to sensitive factors.

The parameter sensitivity identified as the primary factor behind the unsuccessful validation of the pi-OED approach for cycle aging. To ensure comprehensive assessment, the measurement error distribution was examined following the same methodology applied to calendar aging data. Unlike the calendar aging results, the cycle aging data presented in Fig. F.13 demonstrates that the error distribution deviates significantly from normality. This finding indicates that the assumption outlined in remark R2 is not satisfied for the cycle aging experiments, raising important questions about the sensitivity of OED methods to violations of their fundamental mathematical assumptions and prerequisites.
Furthermore, the disparities between bootstrapping and FIM derived variance may be used as an indicator for model validity, parameter estimator convergence, or both.
This study has systematically evaluated the pi-OED approach for LIB aging characterization through a comprehensive multi-stage experimental framework.

ce:enunciation.ce:label: Remark
ce:enunciation.ce:para: The results validate the approaches promised efficiency gains in parameter calibration compared to standard DoE methods, particularly for calendar aging where a 28% reduction in parameter uncertainty was achieved.

ce:section.ce:para: The results confirm that the proposed methods improve parameter calibration efficiency compared to standard DoE approaches, achieving a 28% reduction in parameter uncertainty for calendar aging. However, findings on cycle aging reveal a critical insight: OED effectiveness depends decisively on methodology prerequisites, specifically model adequacy and parameter significance. For cycle aging, where target parameters had non-significant influence on model performance, pi-OED actually increased uncertainty.

The duality of pi-OED becomes apparent — its specificity is both a strength and a potential limitation, as it proves highly susceptible to model quality and parameter sensitivity. These findings underscore the necessity of a structured implementation approach. A sequential framework is recommended: beginning with exploratory experiments using non-model-based designs, followed by rigorous model validation through statistical metrics and residual analysis. Prior to optimization, sensitivity analysis should identify parameters that both exhibit high uncertainty and significantly contribute to model performance — a double criterion essential for selecting appropriate optimization targets. As demonstrated through LOCO analysis of the cycle aging model, evaluating feature importance prevents resource expenditure on optimizing irrelevant parameters. For practical implementation, disparities between bootstrapping and FI-derived variance may serve as indicators for model validity, parameter estimator convergence, or both. However, the general validity of this criterion remains the focus of ongoing research.

Future research directions should prioritize developing robust model identification techniques alongside refined sensitivity analysis methods that better account for parameter inter-dependencies. Furthermore, the method presented here for OED and model parameterization should also be applied to mechanistic aging models that better represent the nonlinear interactions between calendar and cyclic aging [14]. The method is applicable to these parametric models and could deliver even better results if the models describe the measured data more accurately; however, experimental validation requires new test campaigns, as the choice of model influences the experimental design. Adaptive experimental frameworks that dynamically adjust stage durations based on incoming data show particular promise for battery aging characterization. By providing this structured framework and identifying critical prerequisites for successful OED application, this work offers practical guidance for designing efficient battery characterization experiments while highlighting the limitations of model-based optimization when underlying assumptions are not fully satisfied. The importance and sensitivity of all prerequisites for the applicability and optimality of OED techniques and checklist-style thresholds remain the subject of future research.

Florian Stroebl: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Florian Schaeufl: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Oliver Bohlen: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Herbert Palm: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the authors used OpenAI’s chatGPT-4o and Anthropic’s Claude 3 Opus in order to improve readability and quality of language. After using this tools, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the published article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix G
ce:section.ce:para: The validity of a parameterized model can be assessed using several metrics:

ce:list-item.ce:para:
RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error): The RMSE is a measure of the average deviation between observed values and those predicted by the model. It is calculated as the square root of the mean squared error and is expressed in the same units as the dependent variable. A smaller RMSE indicates a better fit of the model to the data. The RMSE is calculated using the formula:
(G.1)
